catalog.title: 58mm Bottomless Portafilter
catalog.summary: Naked (bottomless) portafilter that exposes the basket so you can see channeling and diagnose extraction.
catalog.text: Naked (bottomless) portafilter that exposes the basket so you can see channeling and diagnose extraction. 58mm Bottomless Portafilter A bottomless (naked) portafilter removes the spouts so you can watch the shot pour directly from the basket. It's the best diagnostic tool for channeling: even, mirror like flow means good puck prep; spraying or squirting reveals uneven extraction. Fits all 58mm BrewCraft machines except the entry Pico, which ships a pressurized basket system. Note: a bad puck will spray coffee, so expect some mess while you learn.

catalog.title: Espresso Brew Scale
catalog.summary: Compact 0.1g scale with a built-in shot timer that fits on the drip tray — for weighing dose and yield.
catalog.text: Compact 0.1g scale with a built-in shot timer that fits on the drip tray — for weighing dose and yield. Espresso Brew Scale A compact scale with 0.1g resolution and a built in timer, sized to fit under the portafilter on the drip tray. Weighing your dose (grounds in) and yield (espresso out) is the foundation of repeatable espresso — see espresso fundamentals and dialing in. Auto start timing triggers on first drops. Works with every BrewCraft machine.

catalog.title: Cleaning Tablets (50-pack)
catalog.summary: Backflush cleaning tablets for espresso machines with a three-way solenoid valve — dissolves coffee oils in the group.
catalog.text: Backflush cleaning tablets for espresso machines with a three-way solenoid valve — dissolves coffee oils in the group. Cleaning Tablets (50 pack) Group head cleaning tablets for backflushing — you place one in a blind basket and run the pump to force detergent back through the group and three way valve, dissolving coffee oils you can't reach from outside. See backflushing for the routine. Only for machines with a three way solenoid valve: the Solo, Flux, and Duo. Do not backflush the entry Pico (no solenoid valve) or the Lever — a plain water rinse is the right cleaning for those. Currently out of stock; restocking soon.

catalog.title: Compact Grinder
catalog.summary: Small-footprint conical-burr grinder — forgiving, affordable, and a safe match for any BrewCraft machine.
catalog.text: Small-footprint conical-burr grinder — forgiving, affordable, and a safe match for any BrewCraft machine. Compact Grinder A conical burr grinder with a small footprint and a stepped adjustment ring — the safe, affordable choice that works well with every machine we sell. Conical burrs are more forgiving when dialing in than flat burrs, which makes this a great grinder to learn on. Burr type: conical, 40mm Adjustment: 40 stepped settings Hopper: 150g Footprint: small If you want more clarity and stepless micro adjustment later, step up to the flat burr Vortex or Precision. For a no electricity or travel option, see the Hand Grinder. Compare designs in grinder burrs.

catalog.title: Descaling Solution
catalog.summary: Machine-safe descaling solution that removes limescale from boilers and lines without damaging seals.
catalog.text: Machine-safe descaling solution that removes limescale from boilers and lines without damaging seals. Descaling Solution A citric acid based descaling solution formulated for espresso machines. It dissolves limescale from boilers and water lines without the seal and odor problems that come from using household vinegar. Descale on a schedule set by your water quality — monthly on hard unfiltered water, every 3 6 months on filtered — following descaling & maintenance. Safe for every BrewCraft machine. One bottle is good for roughly 6 8 descales.

catalog.title: 58mm Dosing Cup
catalog.summary: Stainless dosing cup that catches the grind and mates to a 58mm basket for clean, static-free transfer.
catalog.text: Stainless dosing cup that catches the grind and mates to a 58mm basket for clean, static-free transfer. 58mm Dosing Cup A stainless cup sized to a 58mm basket. Grind into the cup (great with single dose grinders like the Vortex or Precision), then invert it onto the portafilter for a clean, low static transfer. Pairs naturally with a WDT tool and puck prep routine.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Duo
catalog.summary: Dual-boiler machine — independent brew and steam boilers for simultaneous pulling and steaming with full temperature control.
catalog.text: Dual-boiler machine — independent brew and steam boilers for simultaneous pulling and steaming with full temperature control. BrewCraft Duo The Duo is our dual boiler machine: one boiler dedicated to brewing, one to steaming, each with its own PID. You can pull a shot and steam milk at the same time with no waiting and no cooling flush, and you can set brew temperature precisely for different coffees — the biggest advantage over a heat exchanger. Boilers: 2 (independent brew + steam), both PID controlled Portafilter: 58mm Simultaneous brew + steam: yes Brew temperature: adjustable, ±1°C stability Recommended for: daily multi drink households If you routinely make several lattes or cappuccinos back to back, the Duo's second boiler earns its price. If you only ever make one drink at a time, you may not need it — the Solo is the better value. Pair with the Precision Grinder for the finest adjustment, or the Compact Grinder on a budget. See the compatibility guide.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Flux
catalog.summary: Heat-exchanger machine — brew and steam at the same time for less than a dual boiler, with a cooling-flush routine.
catalog.text: Heat-exchanger machine — brew and steam at the same time for less than a dual boiler, with a cooling-flush routine. BrewCraft Flux The Flux is a heat exchanger (HX) machine: one large boiler held at steam temperature, with fresh brew water drawn through a tube passing through that boiler on its way to the group. That means you can steam milk and pull a shot at the same time — the big advantage over a single boiler — at a lower price than a true dual boiler. Boiler: single heat exchanger Portafilter: 58mm Steam power: high (large boiler) Simultaneous brew + steam: yes PID: steam pressure only The trade off is brew temperature precision. After the machine sits idle the water in the HX tube overheats, so the first shot needs a short cooling flush — run water through the group for 2 4 seconds until the temperature settles. See Machine Types for how HX compares to single and dual boiler designs. A great pick for latte/cappuccino drinkers who don't want to pay dual boiler prices.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Hand Grinder
catalog.summary: Manual conical-burr grinder with stepless adjustment — capable espresso grind for a small budget, no electricity.
catalog.text: Manual conical-burr grinder with stepless adjustment — capable espresso grind for a small budget, no electricity. BrewCraft Hand Grinder A hand cranked grinder with 38mm stainless conical burrs and a stepless adjustment collar. It grinds fine enough for espresso and is a genuinely good starter option — the main cost is effort (an espresso dose takes 30 45 seconds of cranking). Burr type: conical, 38mm stainless Adjustment: stepless (numbered collar for repeatability) Capacity: single dose (~30g) Power: manual Best paired with the Pico or Solo as a first setup. If you're grinding for espresso daily, an electric grinder like the Compact will save your arm. See the grinder buying guide and grinder burrs explainer.

catalog.title: Knock Box
catalog.summary: Countertop knock box with a padded bar for emptying spent espresso pucks cleanly.
catalog.text: Countertop knock box with a padded bar for emptying spent espresso pucks cleanly. Knock Box A weighted countertop knock box with a rubber padded knock bar for emptying spent espresso pucks. Works with any portafilter size. A simple quality of life accessory for any setup — bang the puck out here instead of into your bin.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Lever
catalog.summary: Spring-lever machine with natural pre-infusion and no electric pump — a tactile, mechanical way to pull shots.
catalog.text: Spring-lever machine with natural pre-infusion and no electric pump — a tactile, mechanical way to pull shots. BrewCraft Lever The Lever is a spring piston machine: you pull the lever down to charge a spring, and the spring drives water through the puck as it releases. There is no electric pump. This gives a natural declining pressure profile and gentle pre infusion, which many drinkers find produces a sweeter, more forgiving shot. Mechanism: spring lever (no pump) Boiler: single, steam capable Portafilter: 58mm Pressure: mechanical, naturally declining from ~9 bar Best for: hands on enthusiasts who enjoy the ritual The Lever rewards good puck prep and a consistent grinder — pair it with the Precision or Vortex grinder. It has a learning curve and isn't the fastest for a morning rush, but it's the most engaging machine we sell. A bottomless portafilter makes it easy to watch your extraction and catch channeling.

catalog.title: 350ml Milk Pitcher
catalog.summary: Stainless steaming pitcher with a precise spout for microfoam and latte art.
catalog.text: Stainless steaming pitcher with a precise spout for microfoam and latte art. 350ml Milk Pitcher A 350ml (12oz) stainless steaming pitcher with a sharp, tapered spout for pouring microfoam and latte art. The 350ml size suits one to two drinks and pairs well with steam capable machines like the Flux and Duo. Fill to just below the spout weld, and see milk steaming for stretching and texturing technique.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Pico
catalog.summary: Ultra-compact entry-level single-boiler machine, forgiving pressurized baskets, ideal first espresso setup.
catalog.text: Ultra-compact entry-level single-boiler machine, forgiving pressurized baskets, ideal first espresso setup. BrewCraft Pico The Pico is our smallest and most affordable machine — built for someone pulling their first shots who wants a low risk way into espresso. It uses a single thermoblock (fast to heat, ~1 minute) and ships with pressurized (dual wall) baskets that are far more forgiving of grind and puck prep mistakes, so you can get a drinkable shot before you've learned to dial in. Boiler: single thermoblock Portafilter: 58mm PID temperature control: no (fixed thermostat) Heat up time: ~60 seconds Footprint: 20 × 25 cm Because it has one heater, you brew first and then steam — expect a short wait to switch modes, like the Solo. When you outgrow the pressurized baskets, drop in a Precision Basket and a real grinder. Not recommended for milk heavy households making several drinks back to back — look at the Flux or Duo instead.

catalog.title: 18g Precision Basket
catalog.summary: Non-pressurized precision filter basket with laser-uniform holes for even extraction at an 18g dose.
catalog.text: Non-pressurized precision filter basket with laser-uniform holes for even extraction at an 18g dose. 18g Precision Basket A non pressurized (single wall) filter basket with laser drilled, uniformly sized holes for even water flow across the whole puck. Sized for an 18g dose — a common target for a double shot at a 1:2 ratio. This is the upgrade from a pressurized starter basket once you've got a real grinder and can dial in. Fits all 58mm machines; the entry Pico can use it once you move off pressurized baskets.

catalog.title: Precision Grinder
catalog.summary: Flagship flat-burr single-dose grinder with 83mm burrs and stepless micro-adjustment — maximum clarity and repeatability.
catalog.text: Flagship flat-burr single-dose grinder with 83mm burrs and stepless micro-adjustment — maximum clarity and repeatability. Precision Grinder Our flagship grinder: 83mm flat burrs with a stepless, high resolution adjustment collar for the finest espresso micro adjustments. Single dose workflow with near zero retention. Built for someone who wants to chase the last few percent of clarity and dial in precisely. Burr type: flat, 83mm Adjustment: stepless, high resolution Workflow: single dose Retention: negligible Flat burrs are less forgiving than conical — they reward good puck prep and a machine with stable temperature. We recommend it for the Duo, Flux, or Lever. It's overkill for the entry Pico or a single boiler only Solo setup, where the Compact or Vortex is a better value match. See grinder burrs and the buying guide.

catalog.title: 58mm Puck Screen
catalog.summary: Thin metal mesh placed on top of the puck to keep the group head clean and improve water distribution.
catalog.text: Thin metal mesh placed on top of the puck to keep the group head clean and improve water distribution. 58mm Puck Screen A 1.7mm stainless mesh screen that sits on top of the coffee puck. It keeps the group head's shower screen clean (less backflushing needed) and can help even out how water first hits the bed. A minor but popular upgrade — it won't fix channeling on its own; good puck prep still matters most.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Solo
catalog.summary: Single-boiler machine with PID temperature control — a compact, precise daily driver for one drink at a time.
catalog.text: Single-boiler machine with PID temperature control — a compact, precise daily driver for one drink at a time. BrewCraft Solo The Solo is a single boiler machine built for one person's daily espresso: small footprint, PID temperature control for shot to shot consistency, and a 3 minute heat up. It steps up from the Pico by adding a real PID and supporting non pressurized precision baskets. Boiler: single, PID controlled Portafilter: 58mm PID temperature control: yes Heat up time: ~3 minutes Recommended for: single users, small kitchens Because it has one boiler, it can't brew and steam simultaneously — you pull your shot, then switch to steam and wait 60 90 seconds for the boiler to reach steam temperature. If you make milk drinks for more than one person in a row, the Flux (heat exchanger) or Duo (dual boiler) will save you that wait. Pairs best with the Compact Grinder for a matching small footprint. See the machine buying guide.

catalog.title: 58mm Tamper
catalog.summary: Flat stainless-steel tamper machined to 58.4mm for a precise fit in standard 58mm baskets.
catalog.text: Flat stainless-steel tamper machined to 58.4mm for a precise fit in standard 58mm baskets. 58mm Tamper A flat stainless steel tamper machined to 58.4mm — a snug fit in a standard 58mm basket that helps you tamp level and consistent. Level tamping is one of the biggest levers against channeling; see puck prep for technique. Fits every BrewCraft machine, all of which use 58mm portafilters.

catalog.title: BrewCraft Vortex
catalog.summary: Single-dose flat-burr grinder with stepless adjustment — clarity-focused espresso grinding with near-zero retention.
catalog.text: Single-dose flat-burr grinder with stepless adjustment — clarity-focused espresso grinding with near-zero retention. BrewCraft Vortex The Vortex is a single dose grinder with 64mm flat burrs and stepless adjustment. You weigh your beans, drop them in, and grind directly into your portafilter or dosing cup — there's no hopper and near zero retention, so every dose is fresh and the beans in the machine never go stale. Burr type: flat, 64mm Adjustment: stepless Workflow: single dose (no hopper) Retention: <0.1g Flat burrs give a more uniform grind and a cleaner, more articulate cup than a conical grinder — see grinder burrs. It's a step below the Precision in build and burr size but shares the single dose, clarity first philosophy. A great match for the Flux, Duo, or Lever.

catalog.title: Water Hardness Test Kit
catalog.summary: Dip-strip test kit for measuring your water hardness so you can set the right descaling schedule and filtration.
catalog.text: Dip-strip test kit for measuring your water hardness so you can set the right descaling schedule and filtration. Water Hardness Test Kit A pack of dip strip tests for measuring your tap water's hardness (GH) and total hardness. Knowing your water tells you how often to descale and whether you need filtration — the Specialty Coffee Association target is roughly 35 85 ppm calcium hardness. See water quality for what the numbers mean and how to adjust. Works for any machine.

catalog.title: WDT Distribution Tool
catalog.summary: Fine-needle Weiss Distribution Technique tool that breaks up clumps for even extraction and less channeling.
catalog.text: Fine-needle Weiss Distribution Technique tool that breaks up clumps for even extraction and less channeling. WDT Distribution Tool A set of fine (0.3mm) needles on a handle, used to stir the grounds in the basket after dosing. This Weiss Distribution Technique breaks up clumps and evens out the coffee bed before tamping — for most home baristas it's the single most effective fix for channeling. Works with any grinder and machine. See puck prep for where WDT fits in the routine.

knowledge.title: Backflushing Your Machine
knowledge.text: A step-by-step guide to backflushing a BrewCraft machine with a three-way solenoid valve to clear coffee oils from the group head. Backflushing Your Machine Backflushing forces water — and optionally detergent — back through the group head and the three way solenoid valve, then out through the valve's drain. This clears coffee oils and fine residue from places you can never reach by wiping. It is a core part of routine maintenance. Backflushing is not descaling These two jobs are constantly confused: Backflushing removes coffee oils from the group head and solenoid valve. Descaling removes mineral scale from the boiler. You need both, on different schedules, and neither replaces the other. Backflushing does nothing for scale; see descaling. Which machines can be backflushed Detergent backflushing only works on machines with a three way solenoid valve , which releases group pressure back through a drain: Solo Flux Duo Do not detergent backflush the Pico (no solenoid) or the Lever (manual group). On those, detergent has nowhere to drain and can be forced back into the pump or boiler. A plain water rinse of the group and basket is the correct routine for them. Check compatibility if you are unsure what your machine has. Detergent backflush (weekly) You need a blind (unperforated) basket . Work through the steps: 1. Remove the normal basket from the portafilter and insert the blind basket . 2. Add one BrewCraft Cleaning Tablet, or about ½ teaspoon of backflush detergent, onto the blind basket. 3. Lock the portafilter into the group and start the brew cycle as if pulling a shot. Water pumps against the blind basket and pressure builds — nothing comes out the bottom. 4. After about 10 seconds, stop the pump. The three way valve releases the built up pressure, pushing cleaner back through the group and out the drain. You will see dirty, soapy water appear in the drip tray. 5. Repeat the on/off cycle 5 8 times, roughly 10 seconds on and a moment off each time, until the water in the tray runs clear with clean foam. 6. Remove the portafilter, take out the blind basket, and rinse it thoroughly to remove all detergent. Reinsert the empty blind basket and run 5 more on/off cycles with clean water and no detergent to flush every trace of cleaner out — residue tastes bad and is not safe to leave. 7. Wipe the group head and shower screen with a clean cloth, then reinstall the normal basket and pull a throwaway shot. Water only backflush A plain water rinse — same blind basket, no detergent — can be done far more often, even daily, to keep the screen and valve clear between detergent cleans. Just run several 10 second on/off cycles with clean water. Note: BrewCraft Cleaning Tablets are currently out of stock . In the meantime, keep up with water only backflushing, or use a compatible backflush detergent at the dose above. Pair this with the wider care routine in maintenance and descaling.

knowledge.title: Buying Guide: Grinders
knowledge.text: How to choose among the BrewCraft Hand, Compact, Vortex, and Precision grinders by budget and the cup you want. Buying Guide: Grinders A good grinder matters as much as the machine — arguably more. It sets the particle size and consistency that every other variable depends on, so if your budget is split, don't starve the grinder. Read grinder burrs for the theory behind these picks. Match the grinder to your needs Budget, travel, or no power → Hand Grinder ($89). Manual 38mm conical, stepless, single dose. Expect ~30 45s of cranking per dose. Compact and quiet. Best value electric, forgiving, works with any machine → Compact ($219). Electric 40mm conical, 40 stepped settings, 150g hopper. The cheapest electric and the easiest to live with; the sweet spot can occasionally fall between two steps. Flat burr clarity plus single dosing at mid price → Vortex ($349). Electric 64mm flat, stepless, near zero retention. A cleaner, more articulate cup than conical. Flagship clarity and finest adjustment → Precision ($429). Electric 83mm flat, stepless high resolution, single dose. Maximum clarity — and the least forgiving, so it rewards good technique. Comparison Model Burr Adjustment Price Best for Hand 38mm conical Stepless $89 Budget, travel, no power Compact 40mm conical 40 stepped $219 Best value forgiving electric Vortex 64mm flat Stepless $349 Flat burr clarity, single dose Precision 83mm flat Stepless $429 Flagship clarity, finest control Pairing advice Pair flat burr grinders with temp stable machines. The clarity of the Vortex or Precision exposes every inconsistency, so they shine on a PID single boiler or dual boiler where brew temperature is steady. Conical grinders are more forgiving and a natural match for a first setup or milk focused drinks. Stepless adjustment helps most on espresso, especially with flat burrs where small moves matter. Check compatibility for basket and dosing cup fit, then work through dialing in for each new coffee.

knowledge.title: Buying Guide: Espresso Machines
knowledge.text: How to choose among the BrewCraft Pico, Solo, Flux, Duo, and Lever by budget and how much milk you steam. Buying Guide: Espresso Machines The right machine depends less on price alone and more on how much milk you steam and how much control you want. Read machine types first for the architecture behind each pick, then match your habits below. Match the machine to how you drink First machine, tight budget → Pico ($249). Single thermoblock, pressurized baskets, ultra compact. Forgiving and cheap; brew then steam if you occasionally want a cappuccino. One person, precise shots, occasional milk → Solo ($349). Single boiler with PID for stable brew temperature and a real 58mm basket. Wait 60 90s between brewing and steaming. Milk drinks for a household, no dual boiler budget → Flux ($549). Heat exchanger brews and steams at once with strong steam. Pull a 2 4s cooling flush after idle; brew temp is a touch less precise. Frequent, back to back milk drinks with best temp control → Duo ($649). Dual PID boilers: simultaneous brew and steam, adjustable brew temperature, no cooling flush. Enthusiast who enjoys the ritual → Lever ($799). Manual spring lever with natural pre infusion and a declining pressure profile. Hands on and steam capable. Comparison Model Type Price Simultaneous brew + steam Best for Pico Single thermoblock $249 No First machine on a budget Solo Single boiler + PID $349 No One person, precise, occasional milk Flux Heat exchanger $549 Yes Household milk drinks, mid price Duo Dual boiler $649 Yes Frequent milk, best temp control Lever Manual lever $799 Yes Enthusiast who enjoys the ritual A few practical notes If you rarely steam milk, a single boiler is no compromise — the money is better spent on the grinder. Steam frequency, not just budget, is the real dividing line: heavy milk use is where HX and dual boiler earn their price. All 58mm models share standard accessories — check compatibility before adding a bottomless portafilter or precision basket. Once you've chosen, get set up with getting started and learn to dial in.

knowledge.title: Compatibility Guide
knowledge.text: Which BrewCraft grinders and accessories pair with each espresso machine. Compatibility Guide Every BrewCraft machine uses a 58mm portafilter , so tampers, baskets, screens, and portafilters are interchangeable across the range by size. The differences come down to which grinder makes sense and which accessories a machine's design supports. Machine matrix Machine Recommended grinders Notable accessories Pico $249 Hand, Compact Ships pressurized baskets; no non pressurized upgrade Solo $349 Compact, Vortex Precision Basket, Bottomless Portafilter, Cleaning Tablets Flux $549 Vortex, Precision Precision Basket, Bottomless Portafilter, Cleaning Tablets Duo $649 Precision, Vortex Precision Basket, Bottomless Portafilter, Cleaning Tablets Lever $799 Precision Precision Basket, Bottomless Portafilter Grinder rules The Compact electric conical works with every machine and is the safe default. The Precision flat 83mm flagship is best on the Duo, Flux, and Lever , where the fine grind control is worth it. It is overkill for the Pico and Solo . The Hand is a low cost entry match for the Pico. See the grinder buying guide and machine buying guide for the reasoning. Baskets and portafilters The Precision Basket and Bottomless Portafilter fit the non pressurized 58mm machines: Solo, Flux, Duo, and Lever . The Pico ships pressurized baskets and is not intended for non pressurized upgrades. Backflushing and cleaning Only machines with a three way solenoid valve can be backflushed: the Solo, Flux, and Duo . The Cleaning Tablets are for those machines. The Pico and Lever are not backflushable. See backflushing. Works with anything The WDT Tool, Knock Box, Puck Screen, 58mm Tamper, Brew Scale, and Water Hardness Test Kit are compatible with every setup.

knowledge.title: Dialing In
knowledge.text: A step-by-step method for finding the right grind setting for any coffee using taste and shot time. Dialing In Dialing in is the process of tuning your grind so a specific coffee tastes its best. Espresso comes down to three numbers: dose (dry grounds in), yield (liquid espresso out), and time (how long the shot runs). You fix the first two and adjust grind until the third lands and the taste balances. The golden rule: change only one variable at a time , and while dialing that variable is almost always grind size. For the why behind these numbers, read espresso fundamentals. The numbered process 1. Set your DOSE and leave it fixed. Weigh your dry grounds — about 18g in an 18g basket. Whatever you pick, keep it identical for every shot while dialing. Changing dose mid session moves the target and hides the effect of your grind changes. Use the Brew Scale for a repeatable dose. 2. Set your target YIELD by ratio. Start at the standard 1:2 — 18g in gives 36g out. Put your cup on the Brew Scale and weigh the liquid espresso, not the cup, not the crema volume. Yield is measured by mass, in grams. 3. Pull a shot and TIME it. Start the timer the moment the pump starts and stop when you hit 36g. Target roughly 25 30 seconds to reach your yield. 4. Taste the shot and read the time together. Too fast (under ~20s) usually tastes sour, thin, and weak — the water rushed through and under extracted, so grind finer to add resistance. Too slow (over ~35s) usually tastes bitter, harsh, and dry — the water dragged and over extracted, so grind coarser to speed it up. 5. Change ONLY the grind between shots. Move one small step at a time. Grinders overshoot easily, so resist big jumps. Stepless grinders like the Vortex and Precision let you nudge grind in tiny amounts, ideal for espresso; the stepped Compact moves in fixed clicks, so if one click runs too fast and the next too slow, split the difference with a small dose or ratio tweak. After each change, run a little coffee through to purge old grounds so you taste the new setting. 6. Re pull and repeat. Keep looping steps 3 5 until the shot both lands near 25 30s and tastes balanced — neither sour nor bitter. Time alone is not the goal; taste is the judge, and time is your guide toward it. 7. Confirm you are dialed in. You are done when you can repeat the same shot three times in a row with consistent time, yield, and taste. Write the setting down. Fresh beans matter Every bean, roast, and even bag behaves differently, so a setting that worked last week may need adjusting today. Coffee is at its best roughly 5 to 21 days after roast. Very fresh beans (under ~4 days) can gush and channel from excess CO2. A new bag always needs re dialing — start from your last known setting and re run the loop. When taste and time disagree If a shot is somehow both sour and bitter, or the time is right but it still tastes wrong, the problem is usually puck prep, not grind — head to puck prep and troubleshooting before chasing the grinder further.

knowledge.title: Espresso Fundamentals
knowledge.text: The three numbers that define every espresso shot — dose, yield, and brew time — and how they shape flavor. Espresso Fundamentals Great espresso is repeatable espresso, and repeatability comes from measuring three things: how much dry coffee you put in, how much liquid espresso you pull out, and how long it takes. Master these and you can control flavor deliberately instead of by luck. The three core numbers Dose is the weight of dry grounds in the basket. For a standard double, most coffees land between 18g and 21g. The dose is set by your basket and your grinder, and you weigh it before pulling. Yield is the weight of liquid espresso in the cup. Always weigh yield rather than eyeballing volume — crema is foam, and it makes volume an unreliable measure. Two shots that look identical in the cup can differ by several grams of actual liquid. Brew time is measured from the moment the pump starts. A typical double runs 25 30 seconds, with roughly 20 40 seconds being an acceptable window depending on the coffee and roast. Brew ratio ties them together Brew ratio is yield divided by dose, written as dose:yield. The standard starting point is 1:2 — 18g in produces 36g out. This is the recipe you should reach for with any new coffee before adjusting to taste. Term What it is Typical value Dose Dry grounds in the basket 18 21g Yield Liquid espresso out (weighed) ~36g for an 18g dose Ratio Yield : dose 1:2 to start Time From pump start 25 30s Under vs over extraction Flavor tells you what the water pulled from the grounds. Under extraction tastes sour, sharp, and thin. The water moved through too fast and left the sweeter, heavier compounds behind. Over extraction tastes bitter, dry, and harsh. The water pulled too much, including the astringent late compounds. Balanced sits in between: sweet, with pleasant acidity and body. Grind size is the main lever you pull to move between these. Finer grind slows the water and raises extraction; coarser grind speeds it up and lowers extraction. Dose and ratio matter too, but you change grind first. Weighing is foundational None of this works without a scale. A good espresso brew scale lets you set dose precisely and stop the shot at your target yield, turning guesswork into a recipe you can repeat every morning. Once you understand these numbers, the practical process of finding the right grind for a given coffee is covered in dialing in. If a shot goes wrong, see troubleshooting.

knowledge.title: Frequently Asked Questions
knowledge.text: Common questions about BrewCraft machines, grinders, compatibility, and policies. Frequently Asked Questions Do I need a grinder? Yes. Espresso depends on a fresh, even, adjustable grind, and pre ground coffee stales within minutes and cannot be dialed in. A dedicated grinder is not optional. See the grinder buying guide. What size portafilter do BrewCraft machines use? All of them use 58mm . That means tampers, baskets, and portafilters are interchangeable by size across the whole range. See compatibility. Can I steam milk and pull a shot at the same time? Only the Flux (heat exchanger) and Duo (dual boiler) brew and steam simultaneously. Single boiler machines like the Solo need roughly 60 90 seconds to switch between brew and steam temperatures. Which machine should a beginner buy? The Pico ($249) for the simplest, most affordable start, or the Solo ($349) if you want PID temperature control and non pressurized baskets to grow into. Compare them in the machine buying guide. How often should I descale? It depends on your water. With hard water, descale about monthly; with filtered or softened water, every 3 6 months. Test first with the Water Hardness Test Kit and read water quality and maintenance & descaling. Can I backflush my machine? Only machines with a three way valve: the Solo, Flux, and Duo. The Pico and Lever cannot be backflushed. See backflushing. Which grinder goes with which machine? The Compact fits everything. The Precision flagship shines on the Duo, Flux, and Lever but is overkill for the Pico and Solo. Full details in compatibility. Do you ship internationally? What is the return policy? See shipping & returns for shipping regions, delivery times, returns, and warranty.

knowledge.title: Getting Started With BrewCraft
knowledge.text: How to assemble your first espresso setup at BrewCraft and where to learn the basics. Getting Started With BrewCraft Welcome to BrewCraft. We sell espresso machines , grinders , and the accessories that tie them together. If you are new to espresso, this page orients you before you buy. What a first setup needs A complete first setup is three things: A machine to brew the shot. A grinder to grind fresh beans for it. A tamper to compress the puck evenly. Add an espresso brew scale as soon as you can. Weighing your dose and yield is the single biggest step toward repeatable shots. The grinder matters as much as the machine Beginners often overspend on the machine and pair it with a cheap grinder. Grind quality and freshness shape extraction more than most machine features do. Split your budget deliberately. Read both guides before deciding: Machine buying guide Grinder buying guide A solid starter combo If you want a recommendation, the Solo ($349, single boiler with PID) paired with the Compact ($219, electric conical) and the 58mm Tamper ($39) is a balanced, affordable start. The Compact pairs with every BrewCraft machine, so it grows with you. For a look at how the machines differ, see machine types. Then learn the fundamentals Gear is only half of it. Once your setup arrives, work through: 1. Espresso fundamentals dose, ratio, and time. 2. Dialing in adjusting the grind to taste. 3. Puck prep distribution and tamping. Shopping with the assistant Our assistant can search the catalog, answer questions about the gear, and add items to your cart for you. Ask it things like "which grinder fits the Flux?" or "add a tamper and a scale." When you are ready, check compatibility to confirm everything works together.

knowledge.title: Espresso Glossary
knowledge.text: Plain definitions of the espresso terms used across the BrewCraft docs. Espresso Glossary A quick reference for the terms used throughout our guides. See dialing in, machine types, and grinder burrs for deeper reading. Dose The mass of dry ground coffee put into the basket, measured in grams. A common single basket dose is 18g. Yield The mass of liquid espresso in the cup. Often around twice the dose for a standard shot. Brew Ratio Dose to yield, written like 1:2 (18g in, 36g out). It is the main lever for shot strength; see dialing in. Extraction How much soluble material the water pulls from the grounds. Under extraction tastes sour and thin; over extraction tastes bitter and harsh. Channeling Water finding a low resistance path through the puck, causing uneven extraction. Good puck prep prevents it; see puck prep. Crema The reddish brown foam on top of a shot, formed by emulsified oils and dissolved CO2. Portafilter The handled holder that carries the basket and locks into the group head. All BrewCraft machines use 58mm. Basket The perforated metal cup holding the puck. Non pressurized baskets rely on your grind and prep; pressurized baskets add a valve to force crema and forgive coarse grinds. PID A temperature controller that holds brew water to a tight, stable setpoint. Heat Exchanger A boiler design that draws brew water through a tube passing through the steam boiler, allowing brewing and steaming at once. See machine types. Dual Boiler Separate boilers for brew and steam, each temperature controlled independently. Single Boiler One boiler that switches between brew and steam temperatures, so you wait between tasks. Pre infusion A gentle wetting of the puck at low pressure before full pressure, promoting even saturation. Burr The grinding element. Flat burrs use parallel discs; conical burrs use a cone in a ring. See grinder burrs. Stepless Adjustment Grind setting with continuous, infinitely fine changes rather than fixed clicks. Retention Grounds left inside the grinder between uses; low retention favors single dosing. Single dosing Grinding one shot's worth of beans at a time instead of filling a hopper. WDT Weiss Distribution Technique: stirring the grounds with fine needles to break clumps before tamping. Tamping Compressing the dose into a level, firm puck. Microfoam Finely textured steamed milk with tiny, glossy bubbles, used for latte art. See milk steaming. Backflushing Cleaning the group by forcing water back through a blind basket, only on three way valve machines. See backflushing. Descaling Removing mineral scale from internal waterways. See maintenance & descaling. TDS Total dissolved solids in water, a measure of mineral content that affects taste and scaling. See water quality. Cooling Flush Running water through a heat exchanger group before brewing to drop overheated water to brew temperature.

knowledge.title: Grinder Burrs Explained
knowledge.text: Flat versus conical burrs and stepped versus stepless adjustment, mapped to the BrewCraft grinder range. Grinder Burrs Explained The grinder shapes the cup as much as the machine does. Two choices matter most: burr geometry (flat vs conical) and adjustment (stepped vs stepless). Conical vs flat burrs Conical burrs use a cone spinning inside a ring. They produce a slightly wider particle size distribution, which tends to give a heavier body, sweeter, more forgiving cup that's easier to dial in. Good for milk drinks and for beginners. Flat burrs use two parallel rings facing each other. Their more uniform grind yields a cleaner, higher clarity, more articulate cup that separates flavors — but the narrower window of good results is less forgiving and rewards accuracy. Conical: Hand Grinder (38mm), Compact (40mm) Flat: Vortex (64mm), Precision (83mm) Stepped vs stepless Stepped adjustment clicks between preset notches. It's simple and repeatable, but for espresso the ideal grind can fall between two steps, which limits fine tuning. The Compact uses 40 stepped settings. Stepless adjustment moves continuously, giving infinite micro adjustment. This matters a great deal for espresso — especially with flat burrs, where a tiny change moves the shot noticeably. The Hand, Vortex, and Precision are all stepless. Single dosing vs hopper, and retention A hopper holds a bean supply for convenience (the Compact's holds ~150g). Single dosing means weighing one shot's beans at a time — flexible for switching coffees, and best paired with low retention so almost no grounds are left behind between doses. The flat burr Vortex is designed for near zero retention single dosing. Comparison Grinder Burr Size Adjustment Feed Hand Conical 38mm Stepless Single dose Compact Conical 40mm 40 stepped 150g hopper Vortex Flat 64mm Stepless Single dose Precision Flat 83mm Stepless Single dose Whichever you choose, expect to dial in each coffee. For a recommendation by use case, see the grinder buying guide.

knowledge.title: Espresso Machine Types
knowledge.text: How single boiler, heat exchanger, dual boiler, and lever machines differ, mapped to the BrewCraft range. Espresso Machine Types The way a machine heats and manages water shapes everything about how it brews and steams. Most pump machines fall into three boiler architectures, with the manual lever standing apart as a mechanical category. Single boiler One boiler heats water for both brewing and steaming. Because espresso wants roughly 90 96°C and steam wants ~130°C+, you cannot do both at once: you brew, flip to steam mode, and wait 60 90 seconds for the boiler to climb before frothing milk. It's the cheapest and most compact approach, and perfectly fine if you drink mostly straight espresso and only occasionally steam milk. Pico — entry single thermoblock, no PID, pressurized baskets. Brew then steam. Solo — single boiler with PID for stable brew temperature, 58mm. Brew, then wait 60 90s to steam. Heat exchanger (HX) A single boiler is held at steam temperature, and cool brew water is drawn on demand through a heat exchanger tube passing inside that boiler. This lets you brew and steam simultaneously with strong steam power. The trade off: brew water temperature drifts upward as the machine idles, so you pull a short cooling flush (2 4s) to reset it before the first shot. Brew temperature is less precise than a PID boiler. Flux — HX design; simultaneous brew and steam, mid price. Dual boiler Two independent boilers, each PID controlled: one for brew, one for steam. You get simultaneous brew and steam and precise, adjustable brew temperature with no cooling flush. It's the most capable and most expensive pump architecture. Duo — dual PID boilers, adjustable brew temp. Lever (mechanical) A separate category. A manual spring lever generates pressure — no electric pump. Pulling the lever compresses a spring that delivers natural pre infusion followed by a declining pressure profile. Steam capable and deeply hands on. Lever — spring lever machine for the enthusiast. Comparison Type Simultaneous brew + steam? Brew temp precision Price tier Single boiler No (mode switch, 60 90s) Low Mid (PID on Solo) $ Heat exchanger Yes Mid (needs cooling flush) $$ Dual boiler Yes High (adjustable) $$$ Lever Yes Mechanical / hands on $$$ Ready to pick? See the machine buying guide, review espresso fundamentals, and check accessory compatibility.

knowledge.title: Maintenance and Descaling
knowledge.text: A routine care and step-by-step descaling procedure that keeps a BrewCraft espresso machine clean, healthy, and tasting good. Maintenance and Descaling A little routine care keeps your machine tasting clean and running for years. There are two separate jobs that people constantly confuse: cleaning coffee oils (a residue you clear by wiping and backflushing) and descaling (removing hard mineral scale from the boiler and lines). Neither replaces the other. This guide covers the full routine and gives you an exact, numbered descaling procedure. Daily / after each use 1. Purge the steam wand with a short burst of steam, then wipe it immediately with a damp cloth so milk cannot dry inside or out. 2. Knock the puck into the knock box and empty the drip tray before it overflows. 3. Rinse the portafilter and basket under hot water to clear oils and fines. 4. Wipe the group head and shower screen with a clean cloth. 5. Flush a few seconds of water through the empty group to rinse grounds off the screen. Weekly 1. Backflush with detergent if your machine has a three way valve (Solo, Flux, Duo). 2. Remove and clean the basket and shower screen to clear built up oils that dull flavour. 3. Check the tank and its filter for scale or slime; rinse as needed. Descaling procedure Scale is mineral buildup left behind by your water. It restricts flow, throws off temperature, and eventually blocks lines. 1. Set your frequency by water quality. Test your supply with the Water Hardness Test Kit. Descale monthly on hard or unfiltered water; every 3 6 months on filtered or soft water. Every BrewCraft machine can be descaled, including the Lever. 2. Empty the water tank, then mix BrewCraft Descaling Solution with fresh cold water following the dilution ratio printed on the bottle. It is citric acid based — never use vinegar, which damages seals and leaves a lingering odour that is very hard to rinse out. 3. Remove the portafilter completely so the solution flows freely straight out of the group head with nothing to restrict it. 4. Run some solution through the group head, then divert and run some through the steam wand so both paths get treated. Stop with roughly half the tank remaining. 5. Turn the machine off and let it soak about 20 minutes. The acid needs dwell time to break down limescale inside the boiler and lines. 6. Run the rest of the solution through, then refill the tank with fresh cold water. 7. Flush the FULL tank of clean water through both the group head and the steam wand to rinse every trace of acid out. If you can still smell or taste solution, run a second full tank of fresh water the same way. 8. Reinsert the portafilter, wipe down, and pull a throwaway shot before serving. Grinder 1. Brush retained grounds out of the chamber and burrs every few weeks. 2. Deep clean the burrs periodically to clear oils and fines that go stale and taste rancid. Quick schedule Frequency Task After each use Purge/wipe wand, empty tray, rinse basket, wipe group Weekly Backflush (if supported), clean basket and shower screen Every few weeks Brush out the grinder Monthly to 6 months Descale, based on water hardness Periodically Deep clean grinder burrs Keep good water going in and most problems never start. If flow or taste has already drifted, check troubleshooting.

knowledge.title: Milk Steaming and Microfoam
knowledge.text: How to steam milk into silky microfoam for lattes and cappuccinos on a BrewCraft machine. Milk Steaming and Microfoam Great milk drinks come down to microfoam : milk textured so finely it pours like wet paint and folds into the espresso — silky, glossy, and integrated, not stiff meringue like foam that sits in a separate layer. Steaming happens in two phases, stretching then texturing, and the whole thing is easiest on steam strong machines. Before you start: machine and milk Steam power decides how forgiving this is. The Flux (heat exchanger) and Duo (dual boiler) can brew and steam at the same time, so you texture milk without waiting. Single boiler machines like the Solo and Pico steam well too, but you must switch modes and wait roughly 60 90 seconds for the boiler to climb to steam temperature — pull your shot first, then switch to steam. Use a cold milk pitcher and cold milk. Whole milk foams most easily thanks to its fat and protein; oat milk is the best plant based option; lower fat and other plant milks work but are less forgiving. The numbered steps 1. Fill the pitcher with cold milk to just below the spout weld. Underfilling gives you too little to work with; overfilling leaves no room for the milk to expand as it aerates. 2. Purge the steam wand briefly into a cloth or drip tray to clear condensed water before the tip touches the milk. 3. Insert the tip just below the surface and open steam fully. Position the wand slightly off center to set up flow. 4. Stretching / aeration phase. Keep the tip near the surface so it makes a gentle, steady "tss tss" hiss — each hiss is a small bite of air introduced into the milk. Aerate about 2 4 seconds for a latte (thin foam) and 4 6 seconds for a cappuccino (more foam). Aerate early, while the milk is still cold; once it warms past body temperature it will no longer take on air smoothly. 5. Texturing / rolling phase. Submerge the tip slightly deeper and angle the pitcher so the milk spins in a whirlpool / vortex . The spin pulls the big bubbles down and shears them into fine microfoam. Keep it rolling until the surface looks smooth and shiny, like wet paint, with no visible bubbles. 6. Stop at 60 68°C (140 155°F). This is where milk tastes sweetest. By feel, the pitcher becomes too hot to hold for more than a second — that is your cue. Never exceed about 74°C (165°F) or the milk scalds and loses its sweetness. A thermometer removes the guesswork. 7. Turn off the steam BEFORE removing the wand. Cutting steam first prevents spraying milk everywhere. Then immediately purge the wand and wipe it with a damp cloth so milk cannot dry inside the tip or on the wand. 8. Tap and swirl. Give the pitcher a firm tap on the bench to pop any remaining large bubbles, then swirl continuously to keep the microfoam glossy and integrated until you pour. Quick reference Cold pitcher, cold milk, filled just below the spout weld. Aerate first (near the surface), then texture (submerged, spinning). Latte ~2 4s air; cappuccino ~4 6s air. Target 60 68°C; hard limit ~74°C. Steam off before wand out; purge and wipe every time. New to your setup? Start with getting started.

knowledge.title: Puck Prep
knowledge.text: How to prepare an even coffee bed — grind, WDT, distribute, tamp — so water flows uniformly through the puck. Puck Prep Puck prep is everything you do between grinding and pulling the shot. Its goal is a uniform bed of coffee that offers the water even resistance everywhere, so the shot flows evenly instead of carving a channel through the weakest point. Channeling ruins a shot that no amount of dialing in can rescue. The order of steps matters, because each one depends on the last — you cannot break up clumps after tamping, and you cannot level a tilt once the puck is compressed. The order Grind → WDT → distribute → tamp → (optional puck screen) → brew The numbered steps 1. Grind into the basket or a dosing cup. Grinding straight into a dosing cup keeps things clean and gives you a contained space to work the grounds before they go in the basket. 2. WDT — the single most effective anti channeling step. Stir the dry grounds with the fine needles of a WDT tool to break up static bound clumps and even out the density of the bed. Move in small circles all the way down to the basket floor, not just across the top. Modern grinders throw clumps that trap air and create weak spots; this stir eliminates them. Do this before you level or tamp — you cannot fix clumps later. 3. Distribute and level the surface. Tap the basket gently or run a distributor across the top so the grounds sit flat and even, with no mound or dip. A level surface going into the tamp gives a level puck coming out. 4. Tamp — level beats forceful. On a flat surface, keep your wrist straight and elbow at roughly 90 degrees , and press straight down with steady, repeatable pressure. Level matters far more than how hard you push: a tilted tamp leaves one side of the puck thinner, and water escapes through that thin edge. Find a pressure you can repeat every shot rather than chasing a number of pounds. Use a tamper matched to your basket — the 58mm tamper fits standard portafilters snugly, leaving no untamped ring at the wall. 5. Optional: add a puck screen. Place a puck screen on top of the tamped bed. It keeps the shower screen cleaner and helps even out the initial flow of water into the puck. It is a refinement, not a substitute for good WDT and a level tamp. 6. Wipe the rim and lock in gently. Brush stray grounds off the basket rim so the portafilter seats cleanly, then lock it into the group without banging it . A knock can crack the puck and cause channeling — the very thing you just worked to prevent. 7. Verify with a bottomless portafilter. Watch the underside as the shot pulls through a bottomless portafilter. A single, even, mirror like stream that merges to the center means your prep worked. Spraying, squirting, or jets shooting sideways mean channeling — go back and revisit your WDT and tamp. Order summary Grind → WDT → distribute → tamp → (screen) → brew. Get the sequence right and the bed does the rest. For a full symptom guide, see troubleshooting.

knowledge.title: Shipping And Returns
knowledge.text: BrewCraft shipping options, delivery times, returns window, and warranty terms. Shipping And Returns Shipping Free shipping on orders over $75 . Orders under $75 ship at a $8 flat rate . Orders leave our warehouse in 1 2 business days . We currently ship within the United States only . This is a demo shop, so international delivery is not available. You will receive tracking by email once your order ships. Returns Return any unused item in its original packaging within 30 days of delivery for a refund. Items must be unused and complete. Used grinders and machines that have brewed or ground coffee cannot be returned as new. Start a return from your order confirmation email, and we will send return instructions. Consumable items such as Cleaning Tablets and Descaling Solution are only returnable if unopened. Warranty Category Warranty Machines and grinders 2 years Accessories 1 year Defective items are replaced free of charge , including return shipping, for the duration of their warranty. Warranty covers manufacturing defects, not wear from normal use or damage from neglected descaling. Questions For anything not covered here, see the FAQ.

knowledge.title: Troubleshooting
knowledge.text: A symptom-to-cause guide with step-by-step fixes for bad espresso shots, separating recipe problems from puck-prep problems. Troubleshooting Most bad shots come down to a handful of causes. The trick is reading the symptom correctly, because the fix for a recipe problem is very different from the fix for a puck prep problem. Taste first, then change only ONE variable at a time — otherwise you can't tell which change did what. Quick diagnosis table Symptom Likely cause Fix Sour, sharp, thin Under extraction Grind finer, lengthen ratio, or brew hotter Bitter, harsh, dry Over extraction Grind coarser, shorten ratio, or brew cooler Sour and bitter at once Channeling Fix puck prep (see below) Spraying/squirting from bottomless Channeling Fix puck prep (see below) Visible holes/craters in the puck Channeling Fix puck prep (see below) Shot too fast (< 20s) Grind too coarse Grind finer Shot too slow ( 35s) Grind too fine Grind coarser Watery, weak Grind too coarse or ratio too long Grind finer or reduce yield No crema Stale beans or grind too coarse Fresh beans, grind finer Under extraction (sour) Sour, sharp, and hollow means the water didn't pull enough — it usually ran too fast. 1. Grind finer to slow the flow and increase extraction. 2. If still sour, lengthen the ratio (pull a little more yield). 3. If still sour, raise brew temperature slightly. Change one lever, re taste, then decide. Work through it methodically in dialing in. Over extraction (bitter) Bitter, dry, and astringent means the water pulled too much. 1. Grind coarser to speed the shot. 2. If still bitter, shorten the ratio (pull a shorter yield). 3. If still bitter, lower brew temperature slightly. If you want to understand why these levers work, revisit espresso fundamentals. Channeling: not a recipe problem Here is the one that fools people. If a shot tastes sour and bitter at the same time , or you see spraying and squirting from a bottomless portafilter, or there are visible holes in the spent puck, you are not looking at an extraction that needs a grind tweak — you are looking at channeling . Water found a low resistance path and blasted through it, over extracting along that channel while under extracting the rest of the bed. That is why you taste both faults together, and why chasing it with grind changes only makes things worse. Fix the bed instead: 1. Use a WDT tool to break up clumps and even out the grounds before tamping. 2. Tamp level and straight down — a tilted tamp leaves a thin edge that channels. 3. Don't thump or tap the portafilter after tamping; it cracks the puck. 4. Consider adding a puck screen on top of the bed. The full method is in puck prep. Shot timing Too fast, under ~20 seconds: grind finer and re pull. Too slow, over ~35 seconds: grind coarser and re pull. Watery, weak, or no crema 1. A thin shot that isn't sour usually means the grind is too coarse or the ratio too long — grind finer or pull a shorter yield. 2. No crema at all usually means stale beans (buy fresh, rested a few days off roast) or, again, a grind that is too coarse. Re taste after each single change, and lean on dialing in to keep your adjustments disciplined.

knowledge.title: Water Quality for Espresso
knowledge.text: Choosing and testing water that tastes good in the cup and protects your espresso machine from scale. Water Quality for Espresso Espresso is roughly 98% water , so what comes out of your tap shapes both the flavour in the cup and the health of your machine. Water that is too hard leaves scale in the boiler; water that is too soft tastes flat and can be corrosive. Targets SCA style guidance gives a workable window for espresso: Measure SCA target Why it matters Total dissolved solids (TDS) ~75 150 ppm Extracts flavour well; too low tastes flat, too high tastes dull Calcium hardness ~35 85 ppm Drives extraction and scale; high hardness builds scale, near zero can corrode Aim for the middle of these ranges for a good balance of taste and machine safety. Testing your water Use the Water Hardness Test Kit dip strips for a quick hardness reading. A TDS meter gives a numeric total dissolved solids figure if you want more precision. Test the water you actually brew with, not a generic figure, since tap water varies by area and season. The scale problem Hard, mineral rich water deposits scale inside the boiler and lines . Over time this causes: Slow or restricted flow Temperature instability Eventual damage to heating elements and valves The fix is prevention: filter the water, and/or descale on a regular schedule set by how hard your water is. Choosing water Filtered tap water is the simplest good option for most people. Remineralised low mineral water gives the most consistent results batch to batch. Avoid distilled or zero TDS water on its own. It tastes flat and can be aggressive toward some boilers, since water with almost no minerals tends to pull them from wherever it can. Dialling in flavour but the water checks out? See dialling in. To keep the machine healthy long term, pair good water with routine descaling.
